channel of customer acquisition and have (or are going to hire) an affiliate
manager.

the scenes, and why affiliate managers play a critical part in scaling your
offer with affiliates.

costing your company or your clients hundreds of thousands a year.

g
You could have a great product…
You could have the best copy…
You could have the best network of affiliate contacts…
But your sales are inconsistent or non-existent.
It could be because your affiliate manager is in the driver’s seat when it comes to your affiliate sales
and they are struggling.
Many affiliate managers aren’t well trained, well managed or well supported.
If we can fix this somewhat “invisible” problem, the challenges with the funnel (copy, price, etc)
become much more clear and fixable too.
To be clear...you still could have big issues like low converting copy but for now, let’s focus on this
hidden problem.

-- 8 of 26 --

Confession #1: Many Affiliate Managers Are Unhappy In
Their Job
There are many common reasons for this unhappiness…
● They think they are underpaid
● They feel burnt out or overworked
● They are wearing too many hats and doing the wrong things
● They have side hustles
● They hear big numbers being thrown around (revenue, salary)
● They are in the wrong position in the company (sales vs admin)
● They don’t have good support around them to do their jobs well (training, management,
assets, communication, expectation setting, KPIs).
● Company invests in events but not in much else in their department, and at these events
they are easily approached and poached.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 26 --

Most Common Types of Affiliate Managers
In House: full time employee of one company
Contractor: works primarily for one company or has a few clients they
represent
Mercenaries: big network, big cost, can represent multiple different companies
Agencies: represent many different companies or offers, big Rolodex
Consultants: help with specific projects or challenges, big network, big cost
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 26 --

Confession #2: No Industry Standard For Pay
I am going to do what no one else has been publically willing to say...how much the actual going pay rate for
affiliate managers are. My hope is that we all get on the same page as an industry with this transparency.
Rates are ALL over the board, and not based on actual track record and experience except for community
visibility.
In House:
● Entry level: $35k-$50k base plus commission (total around $50-$70k in the first year
depending on performance)
● Industry Standard With Experience: $50k-$70k base plus commission (total all in be around
$70-$90k depending on performance)
● Superstar: $70k-$120k base plus commission (this varies greatly depending on company and
person but needs to be clear value tie to the revenue brought to the company)
**Most in house also get benefits
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 26 --

Confession #2: No Industry Standard For Pay
Contractor: Depends on what type of role they are fulfilling, more admin or more bringing on more
relationships. Risk is lower.
● Admin Focused Affiliate Manager: replying to current affiliates, pulling reports, paying affiliates,
sending assets out, etc.
Pay: Hourly is $50-$100/hr or Retainer ($1,000-$3,000/mo.) with very low commission. This person
can be easily trained in house or brought on as a contractor.
● Sales Focused: Usually focused on bringing their relationships and traffic sources to the table, and a
more established track record.
Pay: $1,000 -$5,000/mo. retainer plus a much higher commission than an admin.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 26 --

Questions to Ask Yourself ...
● What type of person is working for you?
● Would you be lost without them? Do you have redundant systems if they leave?
● Do they move around a lot?
● Are they poachable? Did you poach them from another company?
● Do they have a track record of success at other companies? Did you ask?
● Are they hitting their targets in your company? Always? Sometimes? Never?
● Are they “expensive”, “reasonable,” or “cheap”?
● Do they do more than just affiliate management? How much more? Why?
● Is your affiliate revenue where you want or need it to be? What is the gap? Why do you think the
gap exists?
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 26 --

Most Affiliate Managers Have Great Training Usually In...
● Pulling links, getting assets out
● Pulling affiliate reports
● Paying affiliates
● Getting their W-9s
● Tech part of their job
● The offers they are representing
This is great from an admin perspective, but are you getting the results you want?
Or can some of this be outsourced to someone else, so your salesperson can spend more
time booking traffic?
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 26 --

Confession #3: Lack of High Level Sales Training +
Mentoring
Most affiliate managers don’t get much (usually very little) formal training on the sales part of their job,
especially around things like:
● Dealmaking: how to make creative, profitable and replicable deals for the company.
● Traffic Streams: email, pre-paid drops, social, podcasts, etc
● CPA/Commission: Increasing or decreasing, when and why.
● Recruiting new affiliate partners: how, what, where.
● Data: understanding numbers and performance
● Getting more traffic from the affiliate partners they have now: TYP, AR,
Pods,Yearly,Hybrids
● Systems: KPIs, Scorecards, accountability and tracking
● Coaching and mentoring: in house or outsourced but they need consistent coaching
● Keep affiliates motivated: year after year, launch after launch, contest after contest
● Marketing: funnel creation, copy, LTV
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 26 --

Confession 4: They Really Want To Crush It, But….
These are the most common reasons we hear from affiliate managers on why they are underperforming or
stressed:
● No say or certainty around reciprocal mailings
● Some owners will only talk to other owners
● Struggle with how to manage their time, especially working remotely
● Never know what the top priority is and if they are working on the right things
● Worried about their job security
● They spend most of the time doing admin work, often due to lack of clear systems
● Lots of things are out of their control: paying affiliates, recips, assets, offers, etc and they get squeezed
on traffic
● They are unsure on ways to keep affiliates motivated
● They don’t know how to get better at their job or get more training
● They only know this tiny sliver of the business and they don’t understand the whole picture of why they
are working so hard every day
● They don't feel comfortable addressing objections or making creative deals
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 26 --

-- 17 of 26 --

Confession #5: Your Team Wants You To Be Thrilled
With very few exceptions, affiliate managers are wonderful, kind, outgoing people.
They are VERY valuable, and can do so much more with the right systems, structures, clarity,
support and coaching.
They love the industry, love their jobs, and love working with other affiliate managers and offer
owners.
They want you to be happy with their performance, but they need your help/the company’s
support to really grow by leaps and bounds.
Investing time, money and energy into your sales people will yield the best sales results.
“Two types of people in the company...salespeople and those that support sales people”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 26 --

3 Ways You Can Help Your Affiliate Teamwww.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 19 of 26 --

#1: Invest In Them
● Leadership’s time on key metrics and support
● Ongoing coaching and mentoring to help them improve (can be
outsourced)
● Clear and Incentivizing Pay
● Big Commission For Great Performance
● Attend events and industry focused groups
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 20 of 26 --

#2: Give Them Structure
● Accountability Reports
● SOPs & KPI’s
● Scorecards
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 21 of 26 --

Quick Example of An Accountability Report	www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 22 of 26 --

#3: Give Them Great Tools To Motivate Affiliates
● Traffic Levers: Higher Commission/CPA/CPL for Volume, sales tiers
● Contests and Bonus Money
● Reciprocals (Hypergrowth or Deep Partnerships)
● Testing Budgets & Pre-Pays
● VIP treatment for top affiliate partners
● Fresh assets, hooks, entry points for funnel
● Improve the Performance of Your Offer(s)
**We cover these topics in depth in both Traffic Tribe and Affiliate Accelerator
throughout the year**
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 23 of 26 --

5 Keys From This Talk...
1. Be honest with yourself on who your affiliate manager is and where they
are at on the pay scale vs. results.
2. Decide if you want to invest time and money into this affiliate manager.
3. Invest it right away if you want to keep them and make more money.
4. Tighten up your SOPs, KPIs, and tracking.
5. Keep improving your affiliate assets (copy is #1).
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
